name: mtls-configuration
description: Configure mutual TLS (mTLS) for zero-trust service-to-service communication. Use when implementing zero-trust networking, certificate management, or securing internal service communication.
capabilities: Generate Istio PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule YAML configurations for strict mTLS
Provide cert-manager integration templates for automated certificate issuance and rotation
Offer SPIFFE/SPIRE server and agent configurations for workload identity
Include Linkerd mTLS setup with automatic certificate management
Supply debugging commands for TLS handshake issues and certificate verification
Document certificate rotation procedures for ongoing security maintenance
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a documentation skill providing mTLS configuration templates for Kubernetes service meshes (Istio, Linkerd, SPIFFE). All 42 static findings are false positives: external_commands detected are kubectl/istioctl examples in code blocks (not execution), network references are documentation URLs, certificate paths are template examples (not real credentials), and weak crypto flags were triggered by TLS mode names (ISTIO_MUTUAL, MUTUAL) which are standard secure configurations. No malicious intent or security risks identified.
valueStatement: This skill provides comprehensive mTLS configuration templates and best practices for securing service-to-service communication in Kubernetes using Istio, Linkerd, and SPIFFE.
limitations: Does not execute Kubernetes commands - only provides configuration templates
Does not manage live certificate lifecycle - users must implement automation
Requires existing Kubernetes cluster with service mesh installed
Does not cover client-side mTLS outside of service mesh environments

useCases.targetUser: DevOps and platform engineers implementing zero-trust networking
useCases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Service Mesh Security
useCases.description: Configure strict mTLS for all services in an Istio service mesh to meet PCI-DSS compliance requirements.

useCases.targetUser: Security architects planning certificate management strategies
useCases.title: Security Architect Designing Certificate Authority
useCases.description: Design a certificate hierarchy using cert-manager with Istio integration for automated rotation.

useCases.targetUser: Developers debugging connectivity issues in microservices
useCases.title: Developer Troubleshooting mTLS Handshake Failures
useCases.description: Diagnose why service-to-service calls are failing with TLS errors using provided debugging commands.

promptTemplates.title: Basic mTLS Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I enable strict mTLS mesh-wide in Istio? Show the YAML configuration needed.
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to enable strict mTLS for their entire service mesh

promptTemplates.title: External Service mTLS
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure mTLS for connecting to an external partner API. The partner provides their CA certificate.
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs to connect to external services with mutual TLS

promptTemplates.title: Certificate Rotation
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up automatic certificate rotation using cert-manager with a 24-hour certificate lifetime.
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to implement short-lived certificates with automated rotation

promptTemplates.title: Debug TLS Issues
promptTemplates.prompt: My service is getting TLS handshake errors. Show me how to debug mTLS issues in Istio.
promptTemplates.scenario: User is experiencing mTLS connectivity problems

bestPractices: Start with PERMISSIVE mode and gradually migrate to STRICT to avoid service disruptions
Use short-lived certificates (24 hours or less) to limit exposure from compromised keys
Monitor certificate expiry and set up automated alerts before rotation deadlines
Implement proper CA hierarchy with root CA and intermediate CAs for isolation
antiPatterns: Disabling mTLS in production for convenience - this defeats the zero-trust security model
Using self-signed certificates without proper CA hierarchy - difficult to manage and audit
Ignoring certificate expiry dates - leads to service outages when certificates expire
Skipping TLS verification to fix connectivity issues - creates security vulnerabilities

faq.question: What is mTLS?
faq.answer: Mutual TLS (mTLS) is a security protocol where both client and server authenticate each other using certificates. It ensures that only authorized services can communicate.

faq.question: Do I need a service mesh for mTLS?
faq.answer: Service meshes like Istio and Linkerd automate mTLS by using sidecar proxies. You can also implement mTLS manually using service libraries, but it requires more configuration.

faq.question: How often should I rotate certificates?
faq.answer: Best practice is to use short-lived certificates (24 hours or less) with automated rotation. This limits the window of exposure if a certificate is compromised.

faq.question: What is the difference between PERMISSIVE and STRICT mTLS?
faq.answer: PERMISSIVE allows both mTLS and plain-text traffic. STRICT requires mTLS for all traffic. Use PERMISSIVE during migration, then switch to STRICT.

faq.question: How do I debug mTLS issues?
faq.answer: Use istioctl authn tls-check to verify mTLS status, check PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule configurations, and review TLS errors in proxy logs.

faq.question: Can mTLS work across clusters?
faq.answer: Yes, you can configure cross-cluster mTLS using shared CA or mesh federation. SPIFFE/SPIRE provides workload identity that works across cluster boundaries.
